pattern: Since deletions only ever trim the right end, the only strings all three can ever agree on are shared prefixes -- so the longest common prefix directly sets the cheapest target.
hints: Deleting only from the right means every string can only ever shrink down to one of its own prefixes.
The three strings can only become equal at a string that is a prefix of ALL three -- so find the longest prefix shared by all three.

If the very first characters already differ across the three strings, no shared prefix exists and the answer is -1; otherwise the answer is how many characters total must be trimmed to reach that shared prefix.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, impossible from the first character
all three already identical, no deletions needed
common prefix shorter than the shortest string
shared prefix is the entire shortest string

stress.description: random strings sharing a random-length common prefix
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    prefix_len = rng.randint(0, 20)
    prefix = ''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(prefix_len))
    strs = []
    for _ in range(3):
        extra = rng.randint(0, 20)
        suffix = ''.join(rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) for _ in range(extra))
        s = prefix + suffix
        if len(s) == 0:
            s = rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase)
        strs.append(s)
    return strs
